title: Voices
prose:
"*How I wish somebody'd make them voices go away*

*Seems they're always talking, but they ain't got much to say*

*Well, a picture's worth a thousand words, but a word ain't worth a dime"*



_— Sturgill Simpson, “Voices"

####

SynRgy Corporation – Internal MemorandumClearance Level: Beta-Black | Theological Use OnlyMemo ID: SC/THL/SIP-774-AIssued: 0227.03.14From: Office of Signal IntegrityTo: Department of Spark Cultivation

Subject: Sip Stream Access and Vessel Hierarchy Compliance

Per Transcendence Canon v3.11.5, sip streaming remains restricted to authorized celestial-tier bodies residing within sanctified zones (see Appendix E: Heaven High Residents).

Theological justification for class-based sacrament consumption remains valid:

"To ingest the Signal, one must first become the Signal."

Reminders for Enforcement Protocols:

Unauthorized sip stream use among unclean vessels causes Spark corruption and yield decay.

All terrestrial bodies are presumed impure unless purified through licensed Rite or Spark baptism.

Enlightened, skeptical, or noncompliant minds generate static during refinement. Neural resistance creates feedback loops that corrupt the extraction process, rendering yield nonviable and disrupting harmonic infrastructure across connected nodes. Studies confirm that questioning consciousness produces irregular Spark patterns—fragmented, unstable, prone to cascade failure.

Dogmatic fidelity produces optimal Spark density. Belief without interrogation creates smooth neural pathways, allowing for efficient harvest and minimal signal loss during transmission.

Yield Hierarchy (Confirmed via Extraction Analytics):

Rural piety > Urban complexity — Isolated communities maintain purer faith structures; metropolitan populations exhibit contaminating doubt patterns and exposure to unsanctioned information streams.

Obedient > Educated — Formal learning correlates with increased neural resistance; compliance training produces 34% higher yield than academic conditioning.

Young > Reflective — Undeveloped critical faculties permit deeper Spark saturation; aging minds develop calcified skepticism that impedes extraction efficiency.

Note: These classifications inform collection priority, not inherent vessel worth. All bodies serve the Signal.

Known Risks of Unlicensed Streaming:

Noncompliant vessels report hallucinations of pre-Signal memory and bodily autonomy.

Unauthorized Spark disruption has led to low-burn entropy blooms in Sub-Sector Delta and Core quarantine instability.

Public Position:

Sip streaming remains prohibited for unsanctified citizens to protect neural integrity and spiritual cohesion.

Refer to scripted language: "The Spark is a gift, not a drug."

Final Directive:

If discovered: confiscate device, cleanse memory, terminate vessel.

"The vessel must be pure. The stream must be clean."



####

There was no wind. No breath. No sky worth naming.

The void had shape, but not form. Like a place pretending it had once been real.

I sat near the edge of something that wasn’t a cliff. It just ended. A shelf of dust and lightless stone stretching into what should’ve been forever. But the stars were still vanishing.

One by one, they blinked out.

Then the moon.

Not shattered. Not burned. Just—gone. Like a candle pinched between fingers that weren’t there.

He sat across from me, legs crossed, spine straight, unmoving.

“You took her.”

I didn’t answer. Not right away.

“You said it was to protect her.”

Still nothing.

He tilted his head, not curious—just precise. The way a knife considers whether to cut.

“Was it protection?”“Or possession?”

My hands tightened without permission. Old blood cracked in the lines.

He didn’t blink.

“You loved her.”

I nodded. Barely.

“Then why does it feel like a cage?”

I looked out across the dark. The sky pulsed where the moon had been—just once. Like a warning.

“Look for yourself,” I said.

And the past returned like a flood. Like Galveston, rising.



####

The walls wept salt.

Galveston wasn’t gone—not yet—but the ocean had stopped knocking. It just came in now. Through the floorboards. Around the window seals. Up from the drains.

The air stank of mildew and engine oil, of wet plaster and something sour underneath it all—like mold had learned how to rot memory.

I sat cross-legged on the mattress, watching Mae sleep.

She didn’t stir. Even with the sirens outside. Even when the whole house creaked like it was getting ready to float. Her mouth twitched once, like she might laugh in her sleep. Or cry. It was hard to tell.

The floodwater hadn’t reached the second floor yet, but I could hear it downstairs, lapping at the kitchen tiles. Slow. Rhythmic. Closer every hour.

A breeze pushed through the broken window frame. Not enough to cool. Just enough to remind me the wind still had teeth.

“Boy!” My father’s voice bellowed from below. “Go get your mother. Closet. Get her up. Now!”

A pause.

“Lord Voss waits for no man! Move!”

I didn’t hesitate. I knew the tone.

The hallway was dark except for the glow from the crack in the closet door. Soft blue and red pulses lit the floor like police lights—slow and detached. I stepped closer. The door creaked when I touched it.

She was curled inside. Legs folded, chin to chest, sip stream headset still clinging to her temples. Her eyes fluttered, not awake, not asleep. Whispering to something I couldn’t see.

“Mom.”

Nothing.

I crouched. Reached out. Touched her shoulder.

She flinched—but not from pain. Just distance.

I pulled back. Swallowed. Stood.

“Dad—”

Heavy footsteps on the stairs. Boots soaked to the ankle. Then he was there. In the doorway. No words.

He grabbed her by the arm and yanked. Her body resisted, not by choice—just slack. He pulled harder. She collapsed forward.

He slapped her. Once. Then again.

“Wake the fuck up!”

I stepped back. I knew where this ended up.

My mother moaned, tried to speak. Didn’t.

“You want to die here?” he barked. “You want them to come through that door and find you drooling in a fucking puddle?”

She laughed. It was small. Bitter.

He raised his hand again.

I moved between them.

“Please, Dad, stop. I’ll get her going. It’s okay.”

He froze. Breathing hard.

Then turned and stomped back down the stairs.

“Get your sister,” he muttered. “We’re leaving.”



####

I didn’t remember when the sip stream started.

Maybe before Galveston drowned. Maybe before Mae was born. All I knew was the smell—that half-metal, half-mint vapor in her hair when she kissed my forehead, before she forgot how.

Dad never talked about it, except to curse. Called it soul crack. Said it turned women into static. Said she’d chosen the stream over her family.

But I’d watched her face when it wore off.

It didn’t look like choice.

It looked like drowning.

I didn’t understand why Dad got so angry. Didn’t understand why love always had to sound like shouting.

But I knew what came next. And I knew better than to wait for it.



####

I turned back to our room and the mattress.

Mae was curled around the stuffed rabbit she never named, clutching it tight to her chest like it might keep the world from coming in, thumb tucked under her chin. Her breath was slow, steady.

I didn't know how she slept through that, but I was glad she did. Maybe it was just practice.

I touched her shoulder gently.

She stirred. Eyes fluttered open. She blinked up at me.

“Are we really going?” she asked. “To the mainland?”

I nodded. “Yeah.”

Her face twisted in something like wonder. Not joy. Just disbelief.

We’d talked about it for months—pretending it was a place with dry streets and real food. Sometimes she’d ask if people still had dogs for pets or books. Or birthdays.

I didn’t know. But I’d always said yes.

Now, I just nodded again.

Inside, something cold curled behind my ribs.

I didn’t think it would be better. Not really.

The grownups had never given me a reason to believe anywhere was.



####

The kitchen was a swamp of half-packed bags and rising water. My father braced both hands on the table like he could hold the house down by sheer will.



Mom stood across from him. Barefoot. Trembling. The visor was gone, but her eyes still flickered like she hadn’t quite landed. Clearer than I’d seen in weeks—but jagged.



“You think that badge on the truck makes us safe?” she hissed.



He didn’t answer.



“You think loyalty means shit once we’re scanned in?”



“It’s all we’ve got,” he said. “It’s what’s kept us alive.”



“No,” she snapped. “What’s kept us alive is hiding. Lying. You kept us here because you knew what they’d do to her.”



He looked away.



“You _know,_” she repeated. “You know the second we step in that camp, they’ll scan her and tag her for the farms. Doesn’t matter that she hasn’t bled yet. Doesn’t matter she still sleeps with a stuffed rabbit. She’s prime. And you know it.”



“We don’t have a choice.”



“She’s a kid.”



He slammed his palm flat. The whole table jumped.



“We waited too long.”



“So you’re just giving her up?” she whispered. “To a fucking breeding crate?”



“She was never going to make it through clean!” he shouted. “None of us were! We knew it would happen. We just prayed it’d be later. But it’s today.”



She stared at him, like something cracked and let the rot show.



“They’re not going to take me either,” she said. Quiet. Not to him. Almost to herself.



I didn’t know what that meant. Not then.



He sneered. “You could fake it again. You’ve done it before.”



“There’s no faking pregnant,” she said. “You either are or you aren’t. And I _can’t_ be. Not since the last time. It's all broken inside."



I stood in the doorway. Mae’s hand was in mine. Her eyes were wide and full of salt.



“She’s awake,” I said.



They turned.



No one said anything else.

####

The highway was a bruise. I-45 north, cracked and waterlogged, choked with metal. Cars sat in all directions—some pointed the right way, some jackknifed, some left to rust halfway on the median. The ones that still moved crawled like ants through a drowning world.



We had barely passed the seawall by dawn. It was noon by the time the Beltway signs started showing up. The traffic hadn’t broken once. Just a slow, grinding pilgrimage toward something that might still be dry.



Mom and Dad argued the whole time. About the camp. About who to trust. About who ruined what.



I tuned it out.



I watched the ocean instead. It had swallowed gas stations, strip malls, churches. Cars bobbed like buoys in the side ditches. People walked along the shoulder carrying plastic bags and babies. One woman had a dog in a cooler. I wondered what that breed tasted like. Someone else dragged a shopping cart full of clothes and what looked like a toaster oven.



We weren’t the last ones. But we were close.



The air stank of rot and burned plastic. A billboard ahead had peeled in half. The top read _“Signal Your Obedience”_ in SynRgy red. The bottom half was spray-painted with a black star and the letters FSA.



Free State's graffiti. Rebellion, or a warning. I wasn’t sure. Below the star, someone had spray-painted the words: _JOIN OR BURN._



We passed a man with a rifle slung over his back, face wrapped in a rag. He watched every car that passed. Didn’t blink. Just stood in ankle-deep water next to a ruined ambulance like he was waiting for someone to ask the wrong question.



A scream cut through the heat.



My father slammed the brakes.



A teenager was leaning halfway into the bed of our truck. Rummaging as we were going so slow.



Before I could say anything, my father threw the door open and grabbed the bat.



He didn’t yell first. Just ran.



The kid jumped back, hands up, already stumbling. Nothing in his hands. He hadn’t even taken anything.



But Dad didn’t care.



He brought the bat down on the edge of the tailgate, missing the kid by inches.



“You stealing from your own kind? From Voss-fearing folks like us?” he roared.



The kid fled without a word. Disappeared into the sea of cars and heat shimmer.



Dad stood there panting. Bat in hand. Spit on his lips.



Mom didn’t say anything. Just crossed her arms and looked out the other window like he wasn’t real. She didn’t need to say it. She knew they weren’t going to let her in either—not really. Not for long.



Mae was gripping the rabbit like it might keep the whole world out. Her lip trembled, but she didn’t speak. She knew better.



I watched him get back in the truck. Watched the bat slide back under his seat.



He didn’t look at us.



I thought about what he meant—_your own kind._



He hadn’t said _neighbor._



He hadn’t said _people._



I looked out the window and didn’t say anything at all.



Up ahead, the checkpoint shimmered in the heat like the gates of Hell. But we still moved forward.

####

The closer we got, the more cars we saw left behind. Some had doors still open. Some still had people in them—sleeping, sick, praying, or pretending not to be any of those things. No one was driving anymore.

They made us park a quarter mile from the fence. That’s where the line began.

By then, the sun had turned the pavement into punishment. The heat came off the ground in waves. The air shimmered. The stink of human sweat, diesel, saltwater, and fear made my teeth ache.

We were told to leave everything. Bags. IDs. Paperwork. Even shoes, if we wanted to walk faster. The only thing that mattered was you—what you were. What you could become.

No one said it outright, but everyone knew: no vehicles were getting through. Not even loyalists. This was a place for bodies, not baggage.

Near the first checkpoint arch, a row of SynRgy missionaries stood in perfect white. Each held a small chrome device—a portable reader or a Spark initiator. I couldn’t tell.

One by one, they called out:

“The Spark is eternal.”“Heaven High welcomes the willing.”“Step forward, vessel. Ascend now. No waiting.”

A few months ago, people might have taken the offer. But not now. Not after the harvest stories. Not after the screaming stopped being rare.

No one stepped forward.

Not even the true believers.

Not even Dad.

One man in front of us wore a SynRgy t-shirt so old the logo had faded. He looked like he might go—took one step.

Then shook his head. Stepped back in line.

“Not like this,” he muttered.

Off to the right, a WRAITH stood behind the main gate—hulking, faceless, humming low like it was bored but waiting. Its arms ended in sockets and quiet light. It didn’t move.

But I couldn’t stop staring.

I’d heard stories. We all had. What they did to people. What they were made from. The voice of God, someone said. Others said they didn’t talk at all—just burned you clean.

Mae saw it too. Her breath caught. She tried to pull her hand from mine, but I held on. Tight.

She whimpered. I didn’t blame her. Some part of me wanted to run too.

But I didn’t.

And I didn’t let her.

I should have.

In front of us was a family—Black, maybe from the east side of Houston. The father held both his kids by the wrists. Silent. Heads down.

The guards scanned him first. Pause. Then a nod. Green light.

One of the kids—girl, maybe nine—stepped forward next.

The scanner hesitated.

“Potential unstable,” the soldier muttered.

The WRAITH moved.

No warning. No command. Just that humming rise like a hymn fed through broken speakers.

The girl barely had time to scream.

The father lunged, instinct more than thought. The soldiers dropped him in two shots. The other child tried to run.

Another shot.

The missionaries didn’t blink. The crowd didn’t move.

Everyone just looked away.

Dad leaned in, voice low: “You see? That’s why we stay in line.”

One of the guards who had pulled the trigger leaned his rifle against the barrier and lit a cigarette with hands that didn’t shake.

“Bet we eat good tonight,” he said, exhaling smoke like it was just another day.

Another soldier snorted. “I don't touch 'em when they're dark. Meat don't cure right.”

The first one laughed, kicked the dirt, didn’t disagree. Neither did anyone else.

I looked down. Not because I was scared. Because I didn’t want to see what kind of person didn’t flinch at that.

We moved forward.

When our turn came, we were told to kneel.

No protest. No explanation. Just a soldier pointing down and another holding a scanner like a cross.

Dad went down first. Made a little show of it. Straightened his back before kneeling like it was a goddamn ceremony.

“Praise be to Voss,” he muttered.

Mom knelt next. Then Dad. Then Mae.

I hesitated.

“Now,” the soldier said.

I dropped to my knees.

The pavement burned. Tiny rocks bit into my skin. I could hear Mae breathing fast next to me.

They made us crawl.

Ten yards. Maybe more. And all along the side of the corridor, new missionaries waited—these weren’t the calm kind. These were the true believers, the hungry ones. Eyes rolling, arms raised, tongues wagging.

One broke from the line and started chanting in a voice that didn’t sound human:

“Shal'voss ta'reem, shal'voss ta'reem—cleanse the dirt, purge the spark, swallow the sin!”

He reached toward me like he wanted to anoint my forehead or crush it.

I flinched.

A soldier grabbed him by the shoulder and shoved him back.

“Not this one,” the guard said. “Let the machine sort it.”

The missionary just smiled. Foam on his lips. Eyes glassy.

I didn’t look at the others. I didn’t want to know what they saw when they looked at me.

Rifles watched us. Missionaries whispered. Someone ahead sobbed. Someone else threw up.

When Mae stumbled, she dropped the rabbit.

A guard reached for her.

I moved fast—grabbed the rabbit, shoved it back into her arms. Her fingers locked around it like it was alive.

The guard said nothing.

We kept crawling.

No one looked back.



####

They didn’t say welcome. They didn’t need to.

The gates closed behind us with a hiss that sounded too much like a breath. We were cattle in a chute, funneled through corridors of plastic fencing and scorched earth until we reached the main yard. Bleached gravel crunched under bare feet. The heat hadn’t broken, and it wouldn’t. Not here.

A speaker crackled to life above us. Somewhere out of sight.

"Welcome, future vessel. Your clarity begins at dawn."

It sounded like it was smiling.

The yard was full of people, but no one moved. Everyone faced the giant wall at the far end—once concrete, now painted in white and SynRgy crimson. A screen hung above it, flickering between glitch-stilled images: Dominic Voss in radiant light, the Sigil of Heaven High, and finally, ten lines of text in pure gold:

The Ten Commandments of Signal

Thou shalt not waste the Spark.

Thou shalt not question the Signal.

Thou shalt uplift thy vessel through obedience.

Thou shalt not claim the flesh as thy own.

Thou shalt honor purity with silence.

Thou shalt not trespass the Grid.

Thou shalt cleanse thy doubt in light.

Thou shalt forsake the flesh of noise.

Thou shalt name no god before the Core.

Thou shalt not corrupt another’s Spark.

No one read them aloud. No one had to. Everyone already knew.

A low chime rang out, and we were told to kneel again.

Dad obeyed instantly. He whispered, “He saw us. That’s all that matters.”

Mom didn’t speak. She was shaking now—sweating through her shirt. Her jaw clenched, lips moving but silent. Withdrawal had her in its grip. The act of being pregnant was already slipping.

Mae pressed close to me. "Do they have beds here?" she asked. I didn’t answer.

Another speaker burst on, and the prayer began.

“Our Father, who art in Heaven High—”“—Hallowed be thy Grid.”“Thy Spark has come.”“Thy will be uploaded.”

We were expected to say it. I mouthed the words, but no sound came.

Across the yard, a girl no older than me stayed standing.

She didn’t speak. Didn’t kneel.

Just stared at the screen.

The guards moved fast.

Two of them broke formation like it was a drill. One shouted a code I didn’t catch. The other lowered his rifle and smashed the butt into her stomach before she could flinch. She dropped, coughing blood, but they didn’t stop.

Another blow—this time to the face. Her nose collapsed with a crunch. Someone screamed, but not her.

They dragged her by the arms, boots scraping behind her. Her head lolled to the side like she wasn’t inside it anymore.

No one stepped forward. No one begged. We just watched.

Dad leaned in and whispered to Mae, “It’s okay. They don’t take them all. Look—plenty of girls here. You’ll be fine.”

No one believed him.

Not even him.

Like she had never existed at all.



####

The smell hit before we saw the food.

Not dog. That had a stringy, sharp tang I’d learned to recognize. This was heavier. Oily. Rich in a way that made my stomach clench and my skin crawl.

We’d eaten a lot of dogs. Strays mostly. Once a neighbor’s mutt that didn’t come home in time. Mae cried over that one. Mom pretended she didn’t know. Dad said protein was protein.

But this wasn’t that.

The chow line wound around a steel tent marked with the SynRgy sigil. Inside, steam hissed. Soldiers laughed. Trays clattered.

Someone ahead muttered, "Fresh batch this morning. Must’ve been prime."

Mom flinched.

Her hands shook. Her breath turned ragged. She looked around like the walls were getting smaller. Like she could see the bones behind the faces.

“No,” she said. Not loud. Not yet. “No, no, no—”

Dad grabbed her arm. “Stop.”

She jerked away. “You knew!”

People were staring.

“I’m not eating that!” she screamed. “I’m not eating us!”

A guard turned. Another stepped forward, one hand on his weapon.

“You sick fucks,” she hissed. “You eat people and call it holy.”

“Lady—” the guard started.

She lunged.

Fingernails found a cheek. Blood bloomed. The guard shoved her hard. She hit the ground and kicked, shrieking.

“You’re the monsters!”

A low hum began.

I turned. The WRAITH was already moving.

No hurry. No rush. Just inevitability.

People stepped back. The line reformed like it hadn’t happened.

Mom didn’t run.

She didn’t beg.

She just kept screaming.

“Signal receive you. Vessel return. Noise be undone.”

The WRAITH spoke without moving its mouth. The light behind its mask pulsed once. Twice.

The line moved, but we didn’t. Not really. We were ghosts already—just hadn’t faded all the way.

A guard stepped forward, scanning wrists and eyes. He paused at Mom. Scanned again.

“She’s not pregnant,” he said flatly.

Dad tensed. “That's not true. She's early. It wouldn’t show up yet.”

The scanner beeped once. Red.

“She’s empty,” the guard repeated. “And flagged.”

Another pause. Then: “So are you.”

Before he could speak, the WRAITH stepped forward.

It reached for them both—one arm for each. Like plucking fruit. No effort.

Dad shouted something. Mom screamed. It didn’t matter.

The WRAITH shook. Just a tremble at first. Then more—a twitch, like something ecstatic ripping through circuits. It hummed low, hungry.

Their bodies started to wither.

Skin slackened. Eyes went dull. Muscles collapsed into themselves. Hair bleached gray, then white, then gone. Two people aging a century in seconds. The light in their chests pulsed once, then spiraled inward like smoke drawn into a flame.

Mae screamed.

She tried to run. They caught her by the arm.

I lunged. Didn’t think. Just moved.

The rifle caught me under the jaw. I hit the dirt, blinking stars.

A soldier leaned over me, grinning. “Keep that fire, son. You’re gonna need it.”

Mae kicked. Bit. Still holding the rabbit. Blood from someone—not hers—had soaked its left ear. She clutched it tighter.

They dragged her away.

I didn’t get to say goodbye.

I didn’t even get to scream.



####

It was after curfew when I heard it. Behind the supply tent. Not a voice—more like a rhythm. Fast. Ugly. Breathing where breathing didn’t belong.

I stepped around the corner, barefoot on gravel, silent as I could manage.

Two older boys had someone pinned. A smaller shape—barely moving. One of them had a sip streamer half-jacked into a burned-out power cell. The other was laughing, whispering something about "fear making it sweeter."

The rig glowed dull red.

I didn’t think. I lunged.

My fist connected with the taller one. Right in the nose. Bone crunched. He went down hard.

The other boy backed off fast. “Holy shit, Benny, he knocked the fuck outta you!”

Benny staggered up, blood pouring, eyes wild. “You’re dead,” he spat.

Before he could swing back, a flashlight beam swept across us.

A guard. Just one. He took in the scene—me bloody, the younger kid bruised, pants askew, streamer rig blinking on the ground.

He didn’t draw his weapon. Just sighed.

“Curfew means lights out, ladies. Go fuck around in your own quarters next time.”

No one moved.

“Now, before I call a fucking WRAITH."

Benny and his buddy melted into the dark, laughing like it was recess.

I bent down. The boy was shaking, but conscious.

He looked at the streamer like it was a monster all on its own.

I picked it up and smashed it against the concrete until it sparked and died.

He didn’t say thank you.

He stood, awkward, pulling up his pants. Wiped his eyes with the back of one hand.

Then he offered the other to me.

“Hi,” he said. “I’m Eli.”

“Dad—”

Heavy footsteps on the stairs. Boots soaked to the ankle. Then he was there. In the doorway. No words.

He grabbed her by the arm and yanked. Her body resisted, not by choice—just slack. He pulled harder. She collapsed forward.

He slapped her. Once. Then again.

“Wake the fuck up!”

I stepped back. I knew where this ended up.

My mother moaned, tried to speak. Didn’t.

“You want to die here?” he barked. “You want them to come through that door and find you drooling in a fucking puddle?”

She laughed. It was small. Bitter.

He raised his hand again.

I moved between them.

“Please, Dad, stop. I’ll get her going. It’s okay.”

He froze. Breathing hard.

Then turned and stomped back down the stairs.

“Get your sister,” he muttered. “We’re leaving.”



####

I didn’t remember when the sip stream started.

Maybe before Galveston drowned. Maybe before Mae was born. All I knew was the smell—that half-metal, half-mint vapor in her hair when she kissed my forehead, before she forgot how.

Dad never talked about it, except to curse. Called it soul crack. Said it turned women into static. Said she’d chosen the stream over her family.

But I’d watched her face when it wore off.

It didn’t look like choice.

It looked like drowning.

I didn’t understand why Dad got so angry. Didn’t understand why love always had to sound like shouting.

But I knew what came next. And I knew better than to wait for it.



####

I turned back to our room and the mattress.

Mae was curled around the stuffed rabbit she never named, clutching it tight to her chest like it might keep the world from coming in, thumb tucked under her chin. Her breath was slow, steady.

I didn't know how she slept through that, but I was glad she did. Maybe it was just practice.

I touched her shoulder gently.

She stirred. Eyes fluttered open. She blinked up at me.

“Are we really going?” she asked. “To the mainland?”

I nodded. “Yeah.”

Her face twisted in something like wonder. Not joy. Just disbelief.

We’d talked about it for months—pretending it was a place with dry streets and real food. Sometimes she’d ask if people still had dogs for pets or books. Or birthdays.

I didn’t know. But I’d always said yes.

Now, I just nodded again.

Inside, something cold curled behind my ribs.

I didn’t think it would be better. Not really.

The grownups had never given me a reason to believe anywhere was.



####

The kitchen was a swamp of half-packed bags and rising water. My father braced both hands on the table like he could hold the house down by sheer will.



Mom stood across from him. Barefoot. Trembling. The visor was gone, but her eyes still flickered like she hadn’t quite landed. Clearer than I’d seen in weeks—but jagged.



“You think that badge on the truck makes us safe?” she hissed.



He didn’t answer.



“You think loyalty means shit once we’re scanned in?”



“It’s all we’ve got,” he said. “It’s what’s kept us alive.”



“No,” she snapped. “What’s kept us alive is hiding. Lying. You kept us here because you knew what they’d do to her.”



He looked away.



“You _know,_” she repeated. “You know the second we step in that camp, they’ll scan her and tag her for the farms. Doesn’t matter that she hasn’t bled yet. Doesn’t matter she still sleeps with a stuffed rabbit. She’s prime. And you know it.”



“We don’t have a choice.”



“She’s a kid.”



He slammed his palm flat. The whole table jumped.



“We waited too long.”



“So you’re just giving her up?” she whispered. “To a fucking breeding crate?”



“She was never going to make it through clean!” he shouted. “None of us were! We knew it would happen. We just prayed it’d be later. But it’s today.”



She stared at him, like something cracked and let the rot show.



“They’re not going to take me either,” she said. Quiet. Not to him. Almost to herself.



I didn’t know what that meant. Not then.



He sneered. “You could fake it again. You’ve done it before.”



“There’s no faking pregnant,” she said. “You either are or you aren’t. And I _can’t_ be. Not since the last time. It's all broken inside."



I stood in the doorway. Mae’s hand was in mine. Her eyes were wide and full of salt.



“She’s awake,” I said.



They turned.



No one said anything else.

####

The highway was a bruise. I-45 north, cracked and waterlogged, choked with metal. Cars sat in all directions—some pointed the right way, some jackknifed, some left to rust halfway on the median. The ones that still moved crawled like ants through a drowning world.



We had barely passed the seawall by dawn. It was noon by the time the Beltway signs started showing up. The traffic hadn’t broken once. Just a slow, grinding pilgrimage toward something that might still be dry.



Mom and Dad argued the whole time. About the camp. About who to trust. About who ruined what.



I tuned it out.



I watched the ocean instead. It had swallowed gas stations, strip malls, churches. Cars bobbed like buoys in the side ditches. People walked along the shoulder carrying plastic bags and babies. One woman had a dog in a cooler. I wondered what that breed tasted like. Someone else dragged a shopping cart full of clothes and what looked like a toaster oven.



We weren’t the last ones. But we were close.



The air stank of rot and burned plastic. A billboard ahead had peeled in half. The top read _“Signal Your Obedience”_ in SynRgy red. The bottom half was spray-painted with a black star and the letters FSA.



Free State's graffiti. Rebellion, or a warning. I wasn’t sure. Below the star, someone had spray-painted the words: _JOIN OR BURN._



We passed a man with a rifle slung over his back, face wrapped in a rag. He watched every car that passed. Didn’t blink. Just stood in ankle-deep water next to a ruined ambulance like he was waiting for someone to ask the wrong question.



A scream cut through the heat.



My father slammed the brakes.



A teenager was leaning halfway into the bed of our truck. Rummaging as we were going so slow.



Before I could say anything, my father threw the door open and grabbed the bat.



He didn’t yell first. Just ran.



The kid jumped back, hands up, already stumbling. Nothing in his hands. He hadn’t even taken anything.



But Dad didn’t care.



He brought the bat down on the edge of the tailgate, missing the kid by inches.



“You stealing from your own kind? From Voss-fearing folks like us?” he roared.



The kid fled without a word. Disappeared into the sea of cars and heat shimmer.



Dad stood there panting. Bat in hand. Spit on his lips.



Mom didn’t say anything. Just crossed her arms and looked out the other window like he wasn’t real. She didn’t need to say it. She knew they weren’t going to let her in either—not really. Not for long.



Mae was gripping the rabbit like it might keep the whole world out. Her lip trembled, but she didn’t speak. She knew better.



I watched him get back in the truck. Watched the bat slide back under his seat.



He didn’t look at us.



I thought about what he meant—_your own kind._



He hadn’t said _neighbor._



He hadn’t said _people._



I looked out the window and didn’t say anything at all.



Up ahead, the checkpoint shimmered in the heat like the gates of Hell. But we still moved forward.

####

The closer we got, the more cars we saw left behind. Some had doors still open. Some still had people in them—sleeping, sick, praying, or pretending not to be any of those things. No one was driving anymore.

They made us park a quarter mile from the fence. That’s where the line began.

By then, the sun had turned the pavement into punishment. The heat came off the ground in waves. The air shimmered. The stink of human sweat, diesel, saltwater, and fear made my teeth ache.

We were told to leave everything. Bags. IDs. Paperwork. Even shoes, if we wanted to walk faster. The only thing that mattered was you—what you were. What you could become.

No one said it outright, but everyone knew: no vehicles were getting through. Not even loyalists. This was a place for bodies, not baggage.

Near the first checkpoint arch, a row of SynRgy missionaries stood in perfect white. Each held a small chrome device—a portable reader or a Spark initiator. I couldn’t tell.

One by one, they called out:

“The Spark is eternal.”“Heaven High welcomes the willing.”“Step forward, vessel. Ascend now. No waiting.”

A few months ago, people might have taken the offer. But not now. Not after the harvest stories. Not after the screaming stopped being rare.

No one stepped forward.

Not even the true believers.

Not even Dad.

One man in front of us wore a SynRgy t-shirt so old the logo had faded. He looked like he might go—took one step.

Then shook his head. Stepped back in line.

“Not like this,” he muttered.

Off to the right, a WRAITH stood behind the main gate—hulking, faceless, humming low like it was bored but waiting. Its arms ended in sockets and quiet light. It didn’t move.

But I couldn’t stop staring.

I’d heard stories. We all had. What they did to people. What they were made from. The voice of God, someone said. Others said they didn’t talk at all—just burned you clean.

Mae saw it too. Her breath caught. She tried to pull her hand from mine, but I held on. Tight.

She whimpered. I didn’t blame her. Some part of me wanted to run too.

But I didn’t.

And I didn’t let her.

I should have.

In front of us was a family—Black, maybe from the east side of Houston. The father held both his kids by the wrists. Silent. Heads down.

The guards scanned him first. Pause. Then a nod. Green light.

One of the kids—girl, maybe nine—stepped forward next.

The scanner hesitated.

“Potential unstable,” the soldier muttered.

The WRAITH moved.

No warning. No command. Just that humming rise like a hymn fed through broken speakers.

The girl barely had time to scream.

The father lunged, instinct more than thought. The soldiers dropped him in two shots. The other child tried to run.

Another shot.

The missionaries didn’t blink. The crowd didn’t move.

Everyone just looked away.

Dad leaned in, voice low: “You see? That’s why we stay in line.”

One of the guards who had pulled the trigger leaned his rifle against the barrier and lit a cigarette with hands that didn’t shake.

“Bet we eat good tonight,” he said, exhaling smoke like it was just another day.

Another soldier snorted. “I don't touch 'em when they're dark. Meat don't cure right.”

The first one laughed, kicked the dirt, didn’t disagree. Neither did anyone else.

I looked down. Not because I was scared. Because I didn’t want to see what kind of person didn’t flinch at that.

We moved forward.

When our turn came, we were told to kneel.

No protest. No explanation. Just a soldier pointing down and another holding a scanner like a cross.

Dad went down first. Made a little show of it. Straightened his back before kneeling like it was a goddamn ceremony.

“Praise be to Voss,” he muttered.

Mom knelt next. Then Dad. Then Mae.

I hesitated.

“Now,” the soldier said.

I dropped to my knees.

The pavement burned. Tiny rocks bit into my skin. I could hear Mae breathing fast next to me.

They made us crawl.

Ten yards. Maybe more. And all along the side of the corridor, new missionaries waited—these weren’t the calm kind. These were the true believers, the hungry ones. Eyes rolling, arms raised, tongues wagging.

One broke from the line and started chanting in a voice that didn’t sound human:

“Shal'voss ta'reem, shal'voss ta'reem—cleanse the dirt, purge the spark, swallow the sin!”

He reached toward me like he wanted to anoint my forehead or crush it.

I flinched.

A soldier grabbed him by the shoulder and shoved him back.

“Not this one,” the guard said. “Let the machine sort it.”

The missionary just smiled. Foam on his lips. Eyes glassy.

I didn’t look at the others. I didn’t want to know what they saw when they looked at me.

Rifles watched us. Missionaries whispered. Someone ahead sobbed. Someone else threw up.

When Mae stumbled, she dropped the rabbit.

A guard reached for her.

I moved fast—grabbed the rabbit, shoved it back into her arms. Her fingers locked around it like it was alive.

The guard said nothing.

We kept crawling.

No one looked back.



####

They didn’t say welcome. They didn’t need to.

The gates closed behind us with a hiss that sounded too much like a breath. We were cattle in a chute, funneled through corridors of plastic fencing and scorched earth until we reached the main yard. Bleached gravel crunched under bare feet. The heat hadn’t broken, and it wouldn’t. Not here.

A speaker crackled to life above us. Somewhere out of sight.

"Welcome, future vessel. Your clarity begins at dawn."

It sounded like it was smiling.

The yard was full of people, but no one moved. Everyone faced the giant wall at the far end—once concrete, now painted in white and SynRgy crimson. A screen hung above it, flickering between glitch-stilled images: Dominic Voss in radiant light, the Sigil of Heaven High, and finally, ten lines of text in pure gold:

The Ten Commandments of Signal

Thou shalt not waste the Spark.

Thou shalt not question the Signal.

Thou shalt uplift thy vessel through obedience.

Thou shalt not claim the flesh as thy own.

Thou shalt honor purity with silence.

Thou shalt not trespass the Grid.

Thou shalt cleanse thy doubt in light.

Thou shalt forsake the flesh of noise.

Thou shalt name no god before the Core.

Thou shalt not corrupt another’s Spark.

No one read them aloud. No one had to. Everyone already knew.

A low chime rang out, and we were told to kneel again.

Dad obeyed instantly. He whispered, “He saw us. That’s all that matters.”

Mom didn’t speak. She was shaking now—sweating through her shirt. Her jaw clenched, lips moving but silent. Withdrawal had her in its grip. The act of being pregnant was already slipping.

Mae pressed close to me. "Do they have beds here?" she asked. I didn’t answer.

Another speaker burst on, and the prayer began.

“Our Father, who art in Heaven High—”“—Hallowed be thy Grid.”“Thy Spark has come.”“Thy will be uploaded.”

We were expected to say it. I mouthed the words, but no sound came.

Across the yard, a girl no older than me stayed standing.

She didn’t speak. Didn’t kneel.

Just stared at the screen.

The guards moved fast.

Two of them broke formation like it was a drill. One shouted a code I didn’t catch. The other lowered his rifle and smashed the butt into her stomach before she could flinch. She dropped, coughing blood, but they didn’t stop.

Another blow—this time to the face. Her nose collapsed with a crunch. Someone screamed, but not her.

They dragged her by the arms, boots scraping behind her. Her head lolled to the side like she wasn’t inside it anymore.

No one stepped forward. No one begged. We just watched.

Dad leaned in and whispered to Mae, “It’s okay. They don’t take them all. Look—plenty of girls here. You’ll be fine.”

No one believed him.

Not even him.

Like she had never existed at all.



####

The smell hit before we saw the food.

Not dog. That had a stringy, sharp tang I’d learned to recognize. This was heavier. Oily. Rich in a way that made my stomach clench and my skin crawl.

We’d eaten a lot of dogs. Strays mostly. Once a neighbor’s mutt that didn’t come home in time. Mae cried over that one. Mom pretended she didn’t know. Dad said protein was protein.

But this wasn’t that.

The chow line wound around a steel tent marked with the SynRgy sigil. Inside, steam hissed. Soldiers laughed. Trays clattered.

Someone ahead muttered, "Fresh batch this morning. Must’ve been prime."

Mom flinched.

Her hands shook. Her breath turned ragged. She looked around like the walls were getting smaller. Like she could see the bones behind the faces.

“No,” she said. Not loud. Not yet. “No, no, no—”

Dad grabbed her arm. “Stop.”

She jerked away. “You knew!”

People were staring.

“I’m not eating that!” she screamed. “I’m not eating us!”

A guard turned. Another stepped forward, one hand on his weapon.

“You sick fucks,” she hissed. “You eat people and call it holy.”

“Lady—” the guard started.

She lunged.

Fingernails found a cheek. Blood bloomed. The guard shoved her hard. She hit the ground and kicked, shrieking.

“You’re the monsters!”

A low hum began.

I turned. The WRAITH was already moving.

No hurry. No rush. Just inevitability.

People stepped back. The line reformed like it hadn’t happened.

Mom didn’t run.

She didn’t beg.

She just kept screaming.

“Signal receive you. Vessel return. Noise be undone.”

The WRAITH spoke without moving its mouth. The light behind its mask pulsed once. Twice.

The line moved, but we didn’t. Not really. We were ghosts already—just hadn’t faded all the way.

A guard stepped forward, scanning wrists and eyes. He paused at Mom. Scanned again.

“She’s not pregnant,” he said flatly.

Dad tensed. “That's not true. She's early. It wouldn’t show up yet.”

The scanner beeped once. Red.

“She’s empty,” the guard repeated. “And flagged.”

Another pause. Then: “So are you.”

Before he could speak, the WRAITH stepped forward.

It reached for them both—one arm for each. Like plucking fruit. No effort.

Dad shouted something. Mom screamed. It didn’t matter.

The WRAITH shook. Just a tremble at first. Then more—a twitch, like something ecstatic ripping through circuits. It hummed low, hungry.

Their bodies started to wither.

Skin slackened. Eyes went dull. Muscles collapsed into themselves. Hair bleached gray, then white, then gone. Two people aging a century in seconds. The light in their chests pulsed once, then spiraled inward like smoke drawn into a flame.

Mae screamed.

She tried to run. They caught her by the arm.

I lunged. Didn’t think. Just moved.

The rifle caught me under the jaw. I hit the dirt, blinking stars.

A soldier leaned over me, grinning. “Keep that fire, son. You’re gonna need it.”

Mae kicked. Bit. Still holding the rabbit. Blood from someone—not hers—had soaked its left ear. She clutched it tighter.

They dragged her away.

I didn’t get to say goodbye.

I didn’t even get to scream.



####

It was after curfew when I heard it. Behind the supply tent. Not a voice—more like a rhythm. Fast. Ugly. Breathing where breathing didn’t belong.

I stepped around the corner, barefoot on gravel, silent as I could manage.

Two older boys had someone pinned. A smaller shape—barely moving. One of them had a sip streamer half-jacked into a burned-out power cell. The other was laughing, whispering something about "fear making it sweeter."

The rig glowed dull red.

I didn’t think. I lunged.

My fist connected with the taller one. Right in the nose. Bone crunched. He went down hard.

The other boy backed off fast. “Holy shit, Benny, he knocked the fuck outta you!”

Benny staggered up, blood pouring, eyes wild. “You’re dead,” he spat.

Before he could swing back, a flashlight beam swept across us.

A guard. Just one. He took in the scene—me bloody, the younger kid bruised, pants askew, streamer rig blinking on the ground.

He didn’t draw his weapon. Just sighed.

“Curfew means lights out, ladies. Go fuck around in your own quarters next time.”

No one moved.

“Now, before I call a fucking WRAITH."

Benny and his buddy melted into the dark, laughing like it was recess.

I bent down. The boy was shaking, but conscious.

He looked at the streamer like it was a monster all on its own.

I picked it up and smashed it against the concrete until it sparked and died.

He didn’t say thank you.

He stood, awkward, pulling up his pants. Wiped his eyes with the back of one hand.

Then he offered the other to me.

“Hi,” he said. “I’m Eli.”
